Okay. So what do you need to do? You need to position yourself, your product, your services as a solution. Obviously, it's quite pretty straightforward. It needs to be in a compelling way to solve their problems and meet their needs.
Okay. I think that's pretty straightforward. You know what I mean? We all know that you need to find out more about the particular CFO and the company. We've covered this. We've talked about how, you know, leveraging insiders, right. To give you that information. The hero normally will give you that information and help you to get to know—uh, you know— just like you would with the procurement stage, just like you would with operations and with the explorer. Right, you know, you want to do the same with the CFO. Okay, and that's why going back a few slides, I was telling you, you want to be Definitely. Definitely see that even procurement, all these different stages you go through, make friends with everyone in there.
Okay, now, when it comes to CFOs, everything is about you know, an ROI. We've covered this. I don't think I need to educate you. ROI is a return on investment. CFOs are only concerned with return on investment. Right, you know, it starts off with a core issue—right, which is always money. So, you know, and then how much money, how much more money will it take, you know, to make the purchase, you know, of your solution, right? This is what's going through the mind of a CFO. And almost by definition, you know, the ROI will have to be better than other alternatives. Okay, so what are other alternatives? Don't just think ROI as in transactional, like you bring a solution. Oh, we're going to give you this new CRM.
For example, we provide you this new software. We provide you this new coaching for, you know, to it. The ROI has to be calculated. The CFO was always thinking in ROIs and they're thinking exactly. Also at any given time, a company has multiple ROIs and multiple initiatives. But its limited resources. So how do they manage those resources? So sometimes you're not even competing against a competitor. Sometimes you're competing against another initiative altogether. I don't know— I'll just pause there for a second. Think about that, digest that, okay? You know, and sometimes products and services both have ROI, right? You know, so what is the total ownership costs? The total ownership costs for the products? Basically, in other words, you know, what does the product cost above the initial cost?
Is your solution something that could be capitalized? In other words, you know, paid for all at once, or is it expensed? You've heard guile. You know, we talked about this. Right, guile. You know, capex, opex, right? Capitalized, expensed, paid for when you know expense means you know it's paid for when the expenses actually occur. So sometimes, for example, um, you know, you come, you're providing a training, and you know, do they pay up front for the 12-month training, or do they pay every month once you've completed the training? Right. That helps to manage and mitigate for cash flow management for balance sheets. That's what CFOS are interested in. Okay. So capitalizing lower profits at the start, expensing lower profits for period each period, and that expense occurs again.
Sometimes, maybe they're going through different sales cycles, maybe they're going Through different periods, if it's a if it's not been a good quarter, they may not want to be doing expensed during that time. They may want to delay, and they may want to, when you're dealing with procurement, to, you know, different payment terms where it might be 90 days. Why 90 days? Because it's the next quarter. So then they can expense it during the next quarter. Is that making sense? So, you know, the— Oh, go ahead. Yes. I was just going to say, it's commonly known as the TCO, the total cost of ownership. And in tech, a lot of solutions have roughly a three-year lifetime expectancy.
So it's common for CFO to look at a three-year total cost of ownership because they know that in three years, they probably will need to do a refresh of the system so they look at it from a 3D perspective. Yes. Okay. So I'm, you know, we don't have enough time to go into all of this. I just want to give you a bird's eye view. I want to pique your interest. The goal here is, honestly speaking, if you are an AE, if you're in a B2B space, it's not going to happen in just tonight's class. You're going to need to take time. Get to know a CFO. Where do you find them? Interview them. Really find out. You know, if you're at a company, get to know a CFO.
Maybe you don't have access to a CFO. Perhaps even a chartered accountant. A CPA. Right? Even a controller. If you're going to be doing prospecting, don't omit out. This is a critical, critical key secret weapon to all the top, top AEs, and B2B closers. It is that we understand the world of the CFO. The CFO actually can be your friend. So introducing CFO, the ruler, your friend. Now, this is actually Connie, our CFO. And I would be lying to you if I told you that, you know, it's always been a bed of roses with, with Connie. There've been times when I've butted heads with her. There've been times when she can be very challenging. Right. And you know, but that's, that's her role. That's what she does. But sometimes she can be my friend.
And she can be your friend too as well. Or the proverbial Connie for you at your company. If you are closing at an enterprise, a company, get to know the CFO. Take them out for lunch. Go meet with them. Be interested, actually, believe it or not, most CFOs are very busy. You know, you have to time this correctly as well. Uh, be persistent, but be polite and respectful. But eventually, if you are, I can guarantee you, one fast way to make friends with a CFO is you actually take them out for lunch. You offer them to pay for them for their lunch, right? Because they don't want to be, I hate to say they're not cheapskates, but don't say, 'Hey, I will take you out for lunch and let's expense this to the company.' No, don't do that.
That's a surefire way to piss off your own CFO. Say, I'll pay out of my own pocket. And then, during that lunch, when you speak to the CFO, tell them you'd like to learn about, you know, what are the criteria of a CFO? What's important for a CFO, right? Many times, the two departments— don't talk. You're thinking that you have to go externally to close your client's CFO and you need to leverage the sage and the hero to let you know about that company's CFO. But I can guarantee you, you'll get in the door faster if you study and consult with your own CFO, your own finance team internally. And many times in the B2B world, many sales teams will have meetings with the finance team because the finance team will provide, sometimes, some consultation, look at the deal, and they will make an assessment and they'll give the sales team, you know, their perspective on what the client's finance team is going to be likely asking, right?
What are the, you know, for risk assessments, you know, and they will help you to understand and navigate those waters. So when I say the CFO is your friend, it doesn't always mean that it's your own internal finance team. Take them out, let them know the language that's going to make the CFO's ears perk up is, you know, do you mind if I take you out for lunch? I'll pay for it. Not just that part, but do you mind if I ask you, because we're trying to close a $100,000 deal, $200,000 deal, and I'm getting to the stage where I now have to deal with their finance, their CFO. What if you were in that position? What you know what are the critical things that we need to get this deal over the finish line?
And if we can do this, it's going to bring in revenue to our company. CFO is going to love you for that. Okay. Your CFO is going to love you for that. Is this making sense? Okay. So let me just quickly introduce who Connie is. You know, I mean, a quick bio for her. I mean, for the past couple of decades, Connie you know, started off, she rose through ranks very quickly. She became a chartered professional accountant, CPA, and has served—assuming you don't know what a CPA is, do research. There's a difference between bookkeepers, accountants, CPAs. There's different levels of trust in licensing, rights, and in regulatory frameworks. You know, I don't have time to go into all of that. You can do your own research.
So she also served as, you know, in the finance and accounting field for all these years. Recently, you know, Connie you know has worked with— as a CFO for the Vancouver Fire Radius Security. Now, you know, it's a 72-year-old company. Connie's not 72 years old. The company is 72 years old, right? And in her last eight years as CFO in that position, Connie helped them increase their sales by 1. 5 times, right? So that's a substantial improvement when you're talking about a public company, you know, improving their profit level from break-even to double digits year over year. And that's not the only business Connie led. The financial success was attributed to her. She also carried senior financial roles. Sleep Country Canada, DDB Canada, and in many other companies as well, right?
So, you know, now obviously everyone knows Connie is CFO at Dan Lok Organization. Uh, she's known to be fierce. I can tell you she's definitely fierce, honest up front, and of course with her strategic and financial decisions. Uh, you know she she has a lot of analytical abilities, negotiation skills. She developed breakthrough strategies and obviously how to build long-term sustainability and profitability for the companies, okay? So you definitely want to get to know your CFO. Keep in mind, okay? I don't know if many of you realize, but many times the decision-making process is almost equal to a CEO. The CFO is actually there to be a redundancy and to prevent sometimes CEOs from making decisions that may be biased. So it's almost like a partnership.
That uh, you know, sometimes people we always think that the CEO is the head, head, head, and then the CFO is just below, yes and no. In many decisions, the CFO actually has more authority to kibosh and to over-authoritize or to not approve a decision, even if the CEO would like to make it go forward. And the only times that would actually get kibosh is actually if the board, you know, the shareholders, chairman and the board step in, in which then the CFO has to present a case, you know, and actually go against them. Okay, so CFO is a very, very important position in the company. Is this making sense? Yes? Okay. So. Questions not to ever ask a CFO, okay? And these are, these are some of these are pretty common sense ones, but we've seen it happen.
Don't ask a CFO to buy without facts. And this is what most rookie B2B closers do. They go in there. When we say don't ask the CFO, you don't even get to the CFO. And I've heard this from all the other CFOs of other companies. They actually want to approve you. Just give them the facts and the evidence that show these things. If you can do these things, they are happy and glad to give you the approval. CFOS the misconception is that CFOS are tight wads and cheapskates. Actually, contrary to popular belief, they actually wanna drive up revenues. So, if you don't come to them with facts or evidence, if you're not demonstrating to them that you are thinking and already, as if you're an internal member on their team, helping to show that you've done the thinking and the mapping out and the risk mitigation, risk assessment. Then the likelihood of you closing a deal is going to be harder. But if you can demonstrate that.
CFOs know that you're not a CFO—they, you know, so they're not expecting perfect. But they just want to know that you have done the thinking. Does it help increase revenue? Okay. Yes, can you read about yes it's always about risk assessment? Can you read a balance sheet? So don't ask a CFO— you know how soon they want to see the benefits. Okay, you're just shooting yourself in the foot here. All CFOs will say, 'Now.' But they're not unrealistic. They are also pragmatic. So again, this is going to not just in the numbers, but demonstrating timelines, demonstrating best case scenarios, worst case scenarios. Again, you know, being able to present— this is where the business acumen comes from.
Right being able to present to them that you are measuring, and I can tell you, CFOS tend to err more on the pragmatic side. Right, it's their job to be on the pragmatic side. So if you go in there and you're trying to say, 'We can get this done in three months in their mind, they're thinking, 'Sex.' Tell them six. And if we can deliver in three, would that make you happy? They'll say yes, because that's the now, right? Don't ask CFO how much they want to pay for your products or services. Okay, never ask. I can guarantee. Can you guys guess what the answer will be? All CFOs will say, 'As cheap as possible.' Heck, free if possible. But again, CFOs, they're not dumb.
They know that you're a business as well. They know that you also have a finance team. So it's about meeting that middle ground. Okay, so help them to lift their fears and their concerns. Cfos do have fears and concerns. Believe it or not, yes, they do. Right and you want to be able to help them to lift those fears and concerns. How do you do that? Right? Demonstrate through facts, projections, case studies, evidence, impact to a company's cashflow, sales, profit. Cfos or the accountants they're going to be very risk-averse in nature. Right? You know, so this is the golden rule. Right? To not tread the path Okay, where the word 'risk' comes up. If everything that you do has a risk, man, that's a red flag for CFO.
You want to demonstrate to them that you are aware of potential risks and that you're mitigating risks and that's within a manageable, you know, threshold. Right because if you also say there's no risks, then they will think that you are just naive. That's key point there. Okay. Everything comes with a risk by you demonstrating that you've done the thinking about all kinds of risks, market loss, reputation, everything. Always just finance. They are looking at it holistically as a whole. What are the potential risks? I can tell you CFOs will even factor in a meteor coming in and hitting the planet. You know, they're the ones that probably are going, I told you about pandemic. Right, so do do the deep thinking. Obviously, I'm being a little bit exaggeratory here, but they do consider a lot of different things. This is making sense. Yes. So the most effective way to overcome the budget objection, right? You know, we heard the budget objection with Pansy earlier on, right? What do you think it is? What is the best way to overcome the budget objection?
So I'm going to take you through a little bit of a script. And this script actually literally came from Connie. I've done this. I've role-played with her before on this. Use this with some of the B2B closing I've done. So, you know, you basically ask them if a budget is a concern, you know, would the CFO be willing to trade other expenses for this improved economic benefit, one-time investment or ongoing? So let me say this again. Ask. You want to ask them if. The budget is a concern. Would the CFO be willing to trade other expenses? for this improved economic benefit. Many times, like I said, you're not competing with your competitors, you're competing with another. Another initiative that was rolled out gives you an example, a real-life case study.
Um, closing... uh, the CFO at a multinational company in Tokyo, we wanted to roll out the educational product, and they already, you know, they don't have any more budget for this quarter. Right, no more budget even for next quarter. Reason I found out, you know, through asking, was that they actually had rolled out an upgrade of a certain software? But then, as it turns out, there were some delays. So, because of those delays, technically, they're not going to see the true ROI of that software till the end of the year. We're only in Q2 right now. Whereas my program is only one quarter. So, being able to identify that, talking to the CFO, and actually saying, 'Are you able to shift some of the budget, if that if that initiative can be paused?
Are you able to shift some of that budget? Over to our rollout.' And then what will happen is your company will see a faster ROI in a different department, a different area, through the training. Then, when we're complete our program, you can then re-divert the budget back over to that department which is rolling out more of a software which we didn't have any— we're teaching soft skills over here. They're teaching, they're implementing software. So again, it's about shifting and moving the resources at the right time. This is critical. I wasn't a financial specialist at that time, but it was just asking the question. It's no different than in say, like, even B2C closing. And you say, you're closing a real estate agent, and they're spending money.
I'll just make it— very simple, layman's terms. And say, they're spending money on billboards, uh, at bus stops. Okay, and they're putting flyers out. And you're a digital marketing agency. And you say, how much of your money, how much are you spending on ads? Oh, we're spending $1,000 on billboards and $2,000 on sending out letters. Okay, so that's about $3,000. And how much ROI are you getting out of that? And they say, well, you know, we're actually not seeing much ROI. Well, you know, what if I'm not asking you to come up with new budget? I'm asking you, why don't you just shift that $3,000 over, you know, to pay us at the agency. And even conservatively, our agency is able to bring you, you know, an extra $10,000.
So it's about a, you know, just three point three point plus, you know, ROI. Would that make sense? Now you're speaking a CFO's language. Okay now that's overly simplified right when you're dealing with a real estate agent. Take that and apply it to the B2B world. So understanding what's happening in the company. Many times this is where your explorer is going to help you because they're the operations. They know what's happening. Get to know your explorer, the hero, the sage, the explorer is going to help you to find out. So that way you have cards to play. The CFO will actually be very pleasantly surprised and actually impressed that you actually understand that's what's going through their mind. Now, you may not know all the details, but you can ask.
And many times you'll see a CFO, they'll stop and think about it. You know, they may not give you get back to you, but they're definitely thinking about it. Right? Making sense? Yes? Okay, next one. Ask them how much they spend on marketing, how much they spend on legal, how much they spend on most, you know, basically in their P &L. Then ask the CFO if they will consider what you propose— your economic benefits for the product services that you're trying to close. In other words, the CFO is constantly playing with these weighing scales in their mind. They're constantly weighing out. Which one do I put the resources to, which will give me a faster ROI for the company? And they're calculating multiple variables to figure this out.
So as long as you're, it's not never so transactional. Sometimes it is, but you know, most of the time it's not there. You have to. Respect that and if you respect that they're having to juggle and weigh out multiple skills, you know it's not just like left and right— think of like a scale that's like you know, six seven different scales they're managing. Okay, tell them, hey, look, you know, it's only 10% of your overall marketing spend, right? You know, with this, you know, you can look at how much legal expenses you'll be saving or the highest costs are the people costs. Right.
